Definition 1.1 is related to universality as it seeks a broader kind of universality. It is intended to succeed over practical classes of distributions without being tailored to a single target, suggesting a broad and universal approach in contrast to specific targeting.

Our approach is related to anti-concentration as it presents a simple way of checking anti-concentration without relying on moment-matching. This indicates a specific methodological approach to address and evaluate the concentration of data or distributions.

A tester-learner is related to halfspaces as the approach outlined involves designing efficient tester-learners for halfspaces. This design relies on non-convex SGD and builds upon previous works, implying a direct relationship between tester-learners and halfspaces.

A stationary point w is related to the ramp loss as the tester-learner computes w of a smooth version of the ramp loss. This computation is part of the approach to check distributional properties and ensure proximity to the optimal solution w∗.

The unknown marginal D is related to angular distance to w∗ as the tester-learner checks distributional properties to ensure that w is close in angular distance to w∗. This checking process involves analyzing the gradient norm to determine the association between D and the angular distance.
The gradient norm is analyzed in comparison to [GKSV23] for reducing and showing the weak anti-concentration property.
The unit vector is orthogonal to the band T where the width sigma is carefully selected based on specific constraints.

The hypercontractivity property for random variable Z is crucial for the main algorithmic idea.
A sum-of-squares program is used to check the hypercontractivity of random variable Z in the algorithmic approach.
Any D with a bounded Poincaré constant is certifiably hypercontractive in the SOS framework, as stated in the result by [KS17].

λ-nice distributions have two properties related to marginal density qV(x). First, qV(x) satisfies (anti-anti-concentration)qV(x) ≥ 1/λ for any ∥x∥2 ≤ 1/λ. Second, for any two dimensional subspace V, the marginal density qV(x) satisfies a particular property.
The marginal density qV (x) corresponding to the two dimensional subspace V satisfies qV (x) ≤ Q(∥x∥2) for some function Q : R+ → R+

The initial tester exploited the observation that the probability of disagreement between two halfspaces can be upper bounded by a sum of products, where each product has two terms: one corresponding to the probability of falling in a (known) strip orthogonal to w and one corresponding to the probability of having large enough inner product with some unknown vector orthogonal to w, conditioned in the (known) strip
Complications arise due to the fact that conditioning requires rejection sampling.
Complications also arise as rejection sampling requires a lower bound on the probability of falling inside each strip.
